questions.q: What is the primary purpose of an interrupt in embedded systems?
options: To permanently stop the main program execution
To allow the CPU to respond immediately to asynchronous events from peripherals or sensors
To reduce the CPU clock frequency temporarily
To allocate more memory to a running task
questions.explanation: Interrupts enable the CPU to stop executing the current instruction sequence and respond immediately to urgent events (like a button press or sensor trigger) without constantly polling. This is more efficient than polling and enables responsive systems. Interrupts don't stop the program permanently or affect clock/memory.

questions.q: What happens during an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)?
options: The RTOS stops all task scheduling and restarts the system
The CPU executes a special function to handle the interrupt event, then returns to the interrupted task
The interrupt is queued and processed during the next scheduled task
The priority of the interrupted task is immediately lowered
questions.explanation: An ISR is a function that executes in response to an interrupt, handling the event (like reading sensor data or setting a flag). After the ISR completes, execution returns to where it was interrupted. The RTOS can then reschedule based on what the ISR did, but task scheduling doesn't stop.

questions.q: In the context of interrupts, what is 'interrupt latency'?
options: The time an interrupt waits in a queue before being processed
The time from when an interrupt is triggered until the ISR begins execution
The duration of the interrupt service routine itself
The time between two consecutive interrupts from the same source
questions.explanation: Interrupt latency is the delay between an interrupt being asserted (triggered) and the actual execution of the ISR's first instruction. Lower latency is critical for time-sensitive applications. Latency depends on CPU pipeline depth, interrupt masking, and current instruction execution.

questions.q: Why might interrupt nesting be problematic in embedded systems?
options: It prevents the system from handling multiple interrupt sources simultaneously
Nested interrupts (interrupts interrupting ISRs) increase complexity, stack usage, and risk of stack overflow if not carefully managed
Interrupts can only be serviced one at a time by hardware design
Nesting is always the best approach for system responsiveness
questions.explanation: Interrupt nesting allows higher-priority interrupts to interrupt lower-priority ISRs, improving responsiveness for critical events. However, each nested level consumes stack space and increases complexity. Without proper stack allocation, deep nesting can cause stack overflow. Simple systems often disable nesting.

questions.q: What is the main advantage of interrupt-driven I/O over polling?
options: Polling is always faster than interrupts
Interrupts allow the CPU to perform useful work while waiting for events instead of continuously checking
Interrupts require no CPU resources whatsoever
Polling eliminates the need for interrupt handlers
questions.explanation: With interrupt-driven I/O, the CPU executes other code until an event occurs and triggers an interrupt. With polling, the CPU wastes cycles repeatedly checking a flag. Interrupt-driven approaches are more power-efficient and responsive, especially for infrequent events.

questions.q: What does 'interrupt priority' determine in a multi-interrupt system?
options: Which interrupt is processed first when multiple interrupts occur simultaneously
How often an interrupt can occur per second
The size of memory allocated for the interrupt vector table
Whether an interrupt can be masked or not
questions.explanation: Interrupt priority determines which interrupt is serviced first when multiple are pending. Higher-priority interrupts can preempt lower-priority ISRs if nesting is enabled. Priority doesn't control frequency, memory allocation, or maskability—those are separate properties.

questions.q: In PWM (Pulse Width Modulation), what do the frequency and duty cycle control?
options: Frequency controls power and duty cycle controls speed
Frequency controls how often the signal repeats and duty cycle controls the percentage of time the signal is 'on', together controlling power delivery to a load
Both parameters directly control voltage output
Frequency and duty cycle have no effect on the output signal
questions.explanation: PWM frequency determines the switching rate (typically kHz range for many applications), while duty cycle (percentage of on-time per period) determines average power delivered. Together, they allow precise power control via a digital signal. For example, 50% duty cycle delivers half power; 75% delivers three-quarters power.

questions.q: What is the primary difference between a timer interrupt and a periodic interrupt?
options: There is no difference; the terms are interchangeable
A timer interrupt occurs after a programmed time interval expires, while periodic interrupts are pre-planned and automatic
Periodic interrupts can interrupt ISRs, but timer interrupts cannot
Timer interrupts are software-generated while periodic interrupts are hardware-generated
questions.explanation: A timer interrupt fires when the timer counts down to zero after being programmed. Periodic interrupts occur at regular intervals (often using a timer that auto-reloads). All periodic interrupts use timers, but not all timer interrupts are periodic—some fire only once. Both are hardware-generated.

questions.q: Why is keeping ISRs short and fast a best practice in embedded systems?
options: Long ISRs speed up interrupt processing
Short ISRs minimize the time interrupts disable other code, reduce latency for other interrupts, and prevent stack overflow
ISR duration doesn't affect system performance
Long ISRs allow more complex event handling
questions.explanation: Brief ISRs (typically just reading a value and setting a flag) return control quickly, minimizing the impact on other code and interrupts. Complex processing should happen in the main program or RTOS tasks, not in ISRs. Prolonged ISRs increase interrupt latency and consume stack space, potentially causing overflow.

questions.q: In a real-time system, what is 'missed deadline' risk primarily caused by when using interrupts?
options: Interrupts are inherently unreliable and should never be used
High-frequency interrupts consuming excessive CPU cycles, preventing other real-time tasks from meeting their deadlines
Interrupts always prevent deadline misses
The frequency of interrupt occurrence has no effect on system scheduling
questions.explanation: If ISRs or interrupt-triggered processing consume too much CPU time, they can prevent lower-priority real-time tasks from executing and meeting their deadlines. This is especially problematic with high-frequency interrupts (like sensor sampling). Proper load analysis and priority assignment are essential to avoid deadline violations.
